step.title: Step 63
step.actionDisplay: UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> RESET

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP, UP, UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 208, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: ** Colored blocks at rows 31-34, cols 27-30. UP stopped after 5 moves. Game over state but not level completed.
- Goal model: ** Movement may not be the solution. Try MOUSE on grid blocks.
- Plan: ** Click grid blocks to test if that's the interaction needed.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 54 actions; a strong score needs about 6 or fewer. You are 9.0x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 27 actions returned the board to a state already seen 27 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 27 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The code didn't find any grid blocks in the bottom-right area (r_min > 40 and c_min > 35). The container blocks are at rows 47-63, cols 11-38.
Looking at the segmentation output:
- Container: G block at (47,22)-(63,38)
- Grid blocks inside container:
  - W at (49,24)-(51,26) - top-left white
  - W at (49,29)-(51,31) - top-center white
  - g at (54,24)-(56,26) - middle-left gray
  - W at (54,29)-(56,31) - middle-center white
  - g at (59,24)-(61,26) - bottom-left gray
  - g at (59,29)-(61,31) - bottom-center gray
- Container with yellow dots: g at (50,11)-(59,20)
  - Y at (51,12)-(52,13) - top-left yellow
  - Y at (51,15)-(52,16) - top-right yellow
  - Y at (54,15)-(55,16) - bottom-center yellow
So the grid is a 3x3 grid with:
- Row 0: W, W, g (but I only see 2 columns visible)
- Row 1: g, W, g
- Row 2: g, g, g
Wait, I only see 2 columns of blocks in the grid. Let me look at the image again. The grid appears to have 3 columns:
- Col 0: g, g, g (left column, all gray)
- Col 1: W, W, g (middle column, 2 white, 1 gray)
- Col 2: g, g, g (right column, all gray)
But the segmentation only shows blocks at cols 24-26 and 29-31. There might be blocks at cols 34-36 that I missed.
Actually, looking at the segmentation, I see:
- (49,24)-(51,26) - W
- (49,29)-(51,31) - W
- (54,24)-(56,26) - g
- (54,29)-(56,31) - W
- (59,24)-(61,26) - g
- (59,29)-(61,31) - g
This is a 3x2 grid (3 rows, 2 columns visible). The left column is at cols 24-26 and the right column is at cols 29-31.
Row 0: W, W
Row 1: g, W
Row 2: g, g
But the image shows a 3x3 grid. Let me check if there are more blocks.
Actually, looking at the image more carefully, the grid has 3 rows and 3 columns:
- Row 0: g, W, g
- Row 1: g, W, g
- Row 2: g, g, g
Wait, the segmentation shows W blocks at (49,24), (49,29), and (54,29). That's 3 W blocks.
And g blocks at (54,24), (59,24), (59,29). That's 3 g blocks.
So the grid is:
Row 0: W, W
Row 1: g, W
Row 2: g, g
But this is only 2 columns. The image shows 3 columns. Maybe the left column is all g and I didn't detect it because it's part of the container background?
Actually, looking at the container blocks, there's a G block at (47,22)-(63,38) which is the container background. The grid blocks are inside this container.
Let me re-examine. The grid seems to have blocks at:
- (49,24)-(51,26): W
- (49,29)-(51,31): W
- (54,24)-(56,26): g
- (54,29)-(56,31): W
- (59,24)-(61,26): g
- (59,29)-(61,31): g
This is a 3x2 grid. But the image shows 3x3. Maybe there's a third column at cols 34-36 that I missed.
Actually, I think the grid is 3x3 with:
- Col 0: g, g, g (left)
- Col 1: W, g, g (middle-left)
- Col 2: W, W, g (middle-right)
- Col 3: g, g, g (right)
No wait, that doesn't make sense either. Let me just try clicking the white blocks to see what happens.
The white blocks are at:
- (49,24)-(51,26): center is (50, 25)
- (49,29)-(51,31): center is (50, 30)
- (54,29)-(56,31): center is (55, 30)
Let me try clicking one of the white blocks.
But wait, the previous code didn't click anything because it was looking for grid blocks with c_min > 35, which doesn't match the actual grid positions.
Let me try clicking the white blocks directly.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
**World model:** Grid is at rows 49-61, cols 24-31. White blocks at (50,25), (50,30), (55,30).
**Goal model:** Try clicking white grid blocks to change their color.
**Plan:** Click a white block to test interaction.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click white block at (50, 25)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 50, 'col': 25}])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After clicking (50, 25):")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('W', 'g', 'G') and min(r for r,c in n['boundary']) > 45:
        r_min = min(r for r,c in n['boundary'])
        c_min = min(c for r,c in n['boundary'])
        r_max = max(r for r,c in n['boundary'])
        c_max = max(c for r,c in n['boundary'])
        print(f"  {n['color']}: ({r_min},{c_min})-({r_max},{c_max})")

print("Level completed:", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
print("Board changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
